coredata.dc:title: Luminescence of Eu (III) complex under near-infrared light excitation for curcumin detection
coredata.dc:creator: Yuan, Fangzheng
Wu, Xia
Zhang, Hui
coredata.dc:description: An intrinsic Eu(III) luminescence phenomenon of Eu(III) complex was found under near-infrared light (NIRL) excitation of xenon lamp, and the maximum excitation wavelength is about twice the excitation wavelength of its Stokes fluorescence. The NIRL excitation fluorescence was mainly originated from second order diffracted light (SODL) excitation. The Eu(III) complex was consist of Eu(III), Gd(III), 2-trifluoroacetylacetone (TTA) and cetyltrimethylammonium bromide (CTAB). Curcumin (Cur) could notably quench the luminescence intensity of the Eu(III) complex. Based on this, a sensitive method for Cur detection was developed. Under optimum conditions, the decrease extent in the fluorescence intensity at 611 nm exhibited a good linear relationship with the Cur concentration in the range of 2.0 × 10−9 mol/L - 6.0 × 10−8 mol/L under 746 nm excitation, the limit of detection (LOD, S/N = 3) was 5.2 × 10−10 mol/L. While, the linear relationship and the LOD of Stokes fluorescence method (λ ex/λ em = 360/611 nm) were found to be 1.0 × 10−8 mol/L - 6.0 × 10−8 mol/L and 2.6 × 10−9 mol/L, respectively. The former method is superior to the latter one in Cur detection. Both two methods were successfully applied to determine Cur in real samples. The luminescence mechanism of Eu(III) complex under the NIRL excitation and the quenching mechanism of Cur on the Eu(III) fluorescence was also investigated.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Eu(III) complex
Near-infrared light (NIRL) excitation
Second order diffracted light (SODL)
Curcumin

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Natural Science Foundation of Shandong Province
National Natural Science Foundation of China

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A, B) Excitation spectra (λ em = 611 nm). (C, D) Emission spectra. (λ ex = 746 nm, λ ex = 360 nm). Conditions: Eu3+: 5.0 × 10−7 mol/L; Cur: 6.0 × 10−8 mol/L; TTA: 4.0 × 10−5 mol/L; CTAB: 1.0 × 10−4 mol/L; 0.01 mol/L HEPES-NaOH: pH = 8.0; Gd3+: 7.0 × 10−6 mol/L.

(A) Fluorescence spectra at various excitation wavelengths. (B) Eu3+-TTA-CTAB-Gd3+ complex with or without Cur irradiated with a laser pen. (C) CIE 1976 UCS diagrams of Eu3+-TTA-CTAB-Gd3+ (a, c) and Eu3+-Cur-TTA-CTAB-Gd3+ (b, d). Conditions: Eu3+: 5.0 × 10−7 mol/L; Cur: 6.0 × 10−8 mol/L; TTA: 4.0 × 10−5 mol/L; CTAB: 1.0 × 10−4 mol/L; 0.01 mol/L HEPES-NaOH: pH = 8.0; Gd3+: 7.0 × 10−6 mol/L.
UV–Vis absorption spectra. Conditions: Eu3+: 5.0 × 10−7 mol/L; Cur: 1.0 × 10−5 mol/L; TTA: 4.0 × 10−5 mol/L; CTAB: 1.0 × 10−4 mol/L; HEPES-NaOH buffer: pH = 8.0; Gd3+: 7.0 × 10−6 mol/L.
Surface tension curves of the system. Conditions: Eu3+: 5.0 × 10−7 mol/L; Cur: 6.0 × 10−8 mol/L; TTA: 4.0 × 10−5 mol/L; 0.01 mol/L HEPES-NaOH buffer: pH = 8.0; Gd3+: 7.0 × 10−6 mol/L.
RLS spectra. Conditions: Eu3+: 5.0 × 10−7 mol/L; Cur: 1.0 × 10−6 mol/L; TTA: 4.0 × 10−5 mol/L; CTAB: 1.0 × 10−4 mol/L; 0.01 mol/L HEPES-NaOH buffer: pH = 8.0; Gd3+: 7.0 × 10−6 mol/L.

Selectivity of the system. Conditions: Eu3+: 5.0 × 10−7 mol/L; Cur: 6.0 × 10−8 mol/L; TTA: 4.0 × 10−5 mol/L; CTAB: 1.0 × 10−4 mol/L; 0.01 mol/L HEPES-NaOH buffer: pH = 8.0; Gd3+: 7.0 × 10−6 mol/L; CaCl2, ZnSO4, KCl, AlCl3, NaCl, CuSO4, Mg(NO3)2, BaCl2, Na2CO3: 1.0 × 10−5 mol/L; Gly, Asp, Cys, Ala, Glc, UA, Lys, Glu, Tyr: 5.0 × 10−6 mol/L; BSA: 5.0 × 10−7 g/mL; fsDNA: 1.0 × 10−7 g/mL; AA: 1.0 × 10−6 mol/L; FA:1.0 × 10−7 mol/L.

Emission spectra of Eu3+-TTA-CTAB-Gd3+ with various concentrations of Cur, Insets (A) λ ex = 746 nm, (B) λ ex = 360 nm: the plots of ΔI against Cur concentrations: Conditions: Eu3+: 5.0 × 10−7 mol/L; Cur: 2.0 × 10−9 - 6.0 × 10−8 mol/L; TTA: 4.0 × 10−5 mol/L; CTAB: 1.0 × 10−4 mol/L; 0.01 mol/L HEPES-NaOH: pH = 8.0; Gd3+: 7.0 × 10−6 mol/L.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: Conditions: Eu3+: 5.0 × 10−7 mol/L; Cur: 6.0 × 10−8 mol/L; TTA: 4.0 × 10−5 mol/L; CTAB: 1.0 × 10−4 mol/L; 0.01 mol/L HEPES-NaOH buffer: pH = 8.0; Gd3+: 7.0 × 10−6 mol/L.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Comparison with other methods of Cur detection.

row.entry: Nitrogen and sulfur dual-doped carbon nanodots

head.ce:title: Luminescence of Eu (III) complex under near-infrared light excitation for curcumin detection

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Key Laboratory of Colloid and Interface Chemistry (Shandong University), Ministry of Education, Shandong University, Jinan, 250100, People's Republic of China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering
Key Laboratory of Colloid and Interface Chemistry (Shandong University)

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Key Laboratory of Colloid and Interface Chemistry (Shandong University), Ministry of Education, Shandong University, Jinan, 250100, People's Republic of China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Shandong University, Jinan, 250100, PR China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: An intrinsic Eu(III) luminescence phenomenon of Eu(III) complex was found under near-infrared light (NIRL) excitation of xenon lamp, and the maximum excitation wavelength is about twice the excitation wavelength of its Stokes fluorescence. The NIRL excitation fluorescence was mainly originated from second order diffracted light (SODL) excitation. The Eu(III) complex was consist of Eu(III), Gd(III), 2-trifluoroacetylacetone (TTA) and cetyltrimethylammonium bromide (CTAB). Curcumin (Cur) could notably quench the luminescence intensity of the Eu(III) complex. Based on this, a sensitive method for Cur detection was developed. Under optimum conditions, the decrease extent in the fluorescence intensity at 611 nm exhibited a good linear relationship with the Cur concentration in the range of 2.0 × 10−9 mol/L - 6.0 × 10−8 mol/L under 746 nm excitation, the limit of detection (LOD, S/N = 3) was 5.2 × 10−10 mol/L. While, the linear relationship and the LOD of Stokes fluorescence method (λ ex/λ em = 360/611 nm) were found to be 1.0 × 10−8 mol/L - 6.0 × 10−8 mol/L and 2.6 × 10−9 mol/L, respectively. The former method is superior to the latter one in Cur detection. Both two methods were successfully applied to determine Cur in real samples. The luminescence mechanism of Eu(III) complex under the NIRL excitation and the quenching mechanism of Cur on the Eu(III) fluorescence was also investigated.

ce:section.ce:para: Curcumin (Cur), a natural polyphenol in rhizomes of turmeric [1], possesses high physiological and pharmacological activities. In recent years, researches on the biological and clinical medicine have testified that Cur has anti-inflammatory [2], anti-epilepsy [3], anti-cancer [4] and anti-diabetic [5] effects etc. Moreover, Cur has a wide utilization in the foodstuff industry as a yellow pigment [6,7]. The recommended daily intake of Cur as a food additive is 0–3 mg/kg by World Health Organization [8]. It's essential to establish a simple, low-cost and high-sensitivity analytical method for the detection of Cur. Up to now, many methods such as electrochemical methods [7,9], high performance liquid chromatography [10,11], UV–Vis spectrophotometry [12], fluorescence methods [13–16] have been successfully applied to the determination of Cur. The fluorescence method has some advantages of convenient operation, cost-effectiveness and fast response. It is also widely adopted for the detection of Cur separated by HPLC due to its advantages of excellent sensitivity [17,18].

Recently, a new method has been established based on the luminescence excited by near-infrared light (NIRL) excitation of xenon lamp. The excitation wavelength used is about twice of the excitation wavelength of Stokes fluorescence. To date, the luminescence phenomenon is considered to originate from an up-conversion luminescence (UPL) or the second order diffracted light (SODL) excitation luminescence. The researches on this kind of luminescence phenomenon are mainly focus on that of the luminescence carbon dots (CDs) and graphene quantum dots (GQDs) nanomaterials [19,20]. The nanomaterials also have the UPL characteristic under the laser excitation [21,22]. Another important viewpoint about the luminescence phenomenon is considered to be the effect of the second order diffraction from the grating [23–25]. Luo et al. [26] designed a ratiometric sensor of CDs/CoOOH nanosheet upon the intensity ratios of SODL excitation luminescence to first order scattering (FOS) or frequency doubling scattering (FDS) for detecting ascorbic acid. Anyway, the new luminescence method shows high sensitivity and selectivity [19].

Generally, the intrinsic fluorescence of Eu(III) ions shows a large Stokes shift, narrow band emission and long fluorescence lifetime. The fluorescence of Eu(III) has been widely used in sensitive determination of analytes and biological imaging [27,28]. As we know, the Eu(III) fluorescence can be remarkably enhanced by the antenna effect of ligands, rare earth ion co-luminescence effect and sensitizers [29]. Most of the methods upon the fluorescence of Eu(III) complexes are achieved under UV-light excitation. In this work, the Eu(III) intrinsic fluorescence of the Eu3+-TTA-CTAB-Gd3+ complex under the NIRL excitation of Xe-lamp was found. The study on the differences of luminescence properties between the NIRL excitation fluorescence and the Stokes fluorescence of the Eu(III) complex was performed. The two kinds of fluorescence could be quenched by Cur. Based on these facts, the two methods for the determination of Cur were developed and successfully employed to detect Cur in actual samples. In addition, a comparison was made between two proposed methods to illuminate the advantages and disadvantages of the NIRL excitation fluorescence method.

Curcumin was purchased from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Co. Ltd. Eu2O3 (99.99%) and Hexadecyl trimethyl ammonium bromide was purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. Gd2O3 (99.95%) was purchased from Shanghai chemical reagent station. 2-Thenoyltrifluoroacetone and 2-[4-(hydroxyethyl)-1-piperazinyl] ethanesulfonic acid (HEPES) were purchased from Aladdin Industrial Corporation. Eu3+ (2.0 × 10−2 mol/L) or Gd3+ (1.0 × 10−2 mol/L) stock solution was prepared by dissolving Eu2O3 or Gd2O3 in concentrated hydrochloric acid, the solution evaporated to dryness, and then diluted with water. Stock standard solutions of TTA (1.0 × 10−2 mol/L) and Cur (1.0 × 10−3 mol/L) were prepared and diluted with absolute ethanol separately. Ginger powder (Shanghai McCormick Food Co., Ltd) was obtained from local supermarket at Jinan city, China. The other reagents used in the experiment were of analytical grade. Ultrapure water (18.25 MΩ cm) through a 0.22 μm filter membrane was used throughout the whole work.

Fluorescence spectra and fluorescence quantum yield were recorded on a Fluoromax-4 Spectrofluorometer (Horiba, Japan); Resonance light scattering spectra were recorded on a F-7000 fluorescence spectrometer (Hitachi, Japan); UV–vis absorption spectra were performed on a U-4100 UV absorption spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Japan); Fluorescence decay curves were measured on an FLS-920 fluorometer (Edinburgh, Britain); Surface tension were measured on a Force Tensiometer-K100 (KRÜSS, Germany) by hanging plate method. The pH values were measured on a PHS-3C pH-meter (INESA, China). Ultrapure water was obtained from UPT Ultra-pure water system (Ulupure, China).
The fluorescence decay curves of Eu3+-TTA-CTAB-Gd3+ with or without Cur were obtained under the excitation of 360 nm and 746 nm with a microsecond lamp as the excitation light source.

Four curves method was applied to the measurement of quantum yield. The quantum yields of Eu3+-TTA-CTAB-Gd3+ with or without Cur were obtained under the Xe-lamp light excitation of 360 nm and 746 nm, respectively.

In a 25 mL colorimetric tube, Eu3+, Cur, TTA, CTAB, HEPES-NaOH buffer solution and Gd3+ were sequentially added and diluted to 10 mL with water. The mixed solution was incubated for 50 min before measurement. The experimental parameters were set as followed: Scan mode: Synchronous; λ ex = 200 nm, λ em = 200–800 nm; Silt: the excitation and emission slits both were 1.0 nm; PMT voltage: 950 V; Scan speed: 1200 nm/min.

In a 25 mL colorimetric tube, Eu3+ (500 μL, 1.0 × 10−5 mol/L), appropriate amount of Cur, TTA (400 μL, 1.0 × 10−3 mol/L), CTAB (1.00 mL, 1.0 × 10−3 mol/L), HEPES-NaOH buffer (1.00 mL, 0.1 mol/L pH = 8.00), Gd3+ (700 μL, 1.0 × 10−4 mol/L) were sequentially added and diluted to 10 mL with water. The fluorescence was measured after the mixed solution incubated for 50 min. The excitation and emission wavelengths were set as 746 nm and 611 nm, respectively, with both excitation and emission slits of 3.5 nm for the NIRL excitation fluorescence. The Xe-lamp was used as an excitation source throughout. The λ ex/λ em = 360 nm/611 nm, the excitation and emission slits both were 1.0 nm for Stokes fluorescence. The reduction of fluorescence intensity was described with ΔI = I f - I 0, I f and I 0 were the fluorescent signals of Eu3+-TTA-CTAB-Gd3+ complex with and without Cur, respectively.

Sample treatment was carried out according to the methods reported in the literature with slight modification [30]. Ginger powder was dissolved in absolute ethanol and sonicated for 2 h, after centrifugation at 8000 rpm for 15 min, undissolved particles were removed. The supernatant and ethanol washings were transferred to 50 mL brown volumetric flask and diluted to the mark with absolute ethanol. The Cur was determined by the standard addition method according to the proposed analysis methods. The actual samples were assayed in quintuplicate.

Fig. 1 displays the fluorescence spectra of the Eu3+-TTA-CTAB-Gd3+ system. The strong intrinsic fluorescence of Eu(III) at 594 nm (5D0→7F1), 611 nm (5D0→7F2), 651 nm (5D0→7F3), and 700 nm (5D0→7F4) were obtained in the presence of TTA, CTAB and Gd3+. The strongest emission peak was located at 611 nm. And two different excitation wavelength regions were observed in ultraviolet region and in the near infrared region, respectively. The excitation wavelength in the near infrared region was obviously larger than the emission wavelength of Eu(III). To investigate this kind of luminescent characteristic, we measured the fluorescence spectra of the system at various excitation wavelengths from 706 nm to 796 nm (Fig. 2 A). The results showed that the fluorescence peak positions basically unchanged, and the strongest fluorescence was obtained under 746 nm excitation. As we known, when the excitation wavelength is set as λ nm, the weaker λ/2 nm beam component can also be detected [23]. Considering the wavelength of the NIRL excitation fluorescence is about two times that of Stokes fluorescence, we placed a long wave pass filter in the excitation light path of the spectrofluorometer to eliminate the effect of SODL. The results showed that intrinsic fluorescence of Eu(III) was not basically observed. We think that the fluorescence obtained using NIRL excitation is mainly attributed to SODL excitation luminescence. Furthermore, under the same experiment condition, the fluorescence intensity of the system excited at 360 nm and 746 nm was measured. The fluorescence intensity at 611 nm under 360 nm excitation was about 160 times of that under 746 nm excitation. The photos of Eu(III) complexes solution with and without Cur under a laser pen (Deli, China) irradiating were showed in Fig. 2B. The red beam is mainly attributed to the Tyndall effect of the system. And it could be found that the brightness of solution and red beam was weaker in the presence of Cur. This phenomenon indicated that Cur may have influence on the dispersed state of the system. Examination of the CIE 1976 uniform chromaticity scale (UCS) diagram of the complex in Fig. 2C revealed that the abscissa value of CIE UCS diagram at 746 nm excitation was smaller than that of at 360 nm excitation, while the ordinate value was larger. In one word, the system emits red light at both excitation wavelengths, but its color also shows slight differences. As Fig. S1 shown, the fluorescence decay curves at 746 nm and 360 nm excitations were both suitable for double exponential fitting. The fluorescence lifetime (τ) of the system were 305.83 (33.37%, τ 1) and 600.81 (66.27%, τ 2) at 746 nm excitation, and 252.10 (29.63%, τ 1) and 598.70 (70.37%, τ 2) at 360 nm excitation, respectively. The τ 1 and τ 2 of the former both longer than that of the latter. While the τ 2/τ 1 ratio of the latter was bigger. The results suggested that there are notable differences in the fluorescence characteristics at two wavelength excitations. We deduce that the fluorescence of the system obtained using 746 nm excitation maybe involve other excitation mode. A further improvement research is deserved.

The fluorescence spectra of the systems of Eu3+-TTA, Eu3+-TTA-CTAB, Eu3+-TTA-CTAB-Gd3+ and Eu3+-Cur-TTA-CTAB-Gd3+ were shown in Fig. 1. As we know, the emission peaks at 594 nm (5D0→7F1) and 611 nm (5D0→7F2) are the magnetic dipole (MD) and electric dipole (ED) transition of Eu3+ ions, respectively. The ED transition is also known as hypersensitive transition which is more sensitive to the local symmetry around Eu3+ ion [31]. Their integrated intensity ratio
R =
I
(
D 0
5
→
F 2
7
)
/
I
(
D 0
5
→
F 1
7
)
is often used as a measure of the local symmetry around Eu3+ ion. The larger the R, the lower the local symmetry around Eu3+ ion [32]. Here, the change of R value was adopted to investigate the quenching mechanism of Cur on the fluorescence of Eu(III) complex. The comparison of the emission parameters (
I
D 0
5
→
F 2
7
,
I
(
D 0
5
→
F 1
7
)
and R) of the system are shown in Table S1. We found that at λ ex = 360 nm, the R value of Eu3+-TTA and Eu3+-TTA-CTAB were 7.22 and 27.11, respectively. It indicates that CTAB can greatly increase the local asymmetry around Eu3+ ion. While, the R value of Eu3+-TTA-CTAB-Gd3+ was 28.06. The little change of the R value by added Gd3+ indicates that the combination of Gd3+ with TTA has less effect on the local symmetry around Eu3+ ion. Thus, the notably co-luminescence effect is mainly due to the intermolecular energy transfer from Gd3+-TTA complex to Eu3+ and energy insulating shell of Gd3+-TTA-CTAB complex, rather than the change of the local symmetry around Eu3+ ion. The R value of Eu3+-Cur-TTA-CTAB-Gd3+ was 27.48, which was a slight decrease compared with that of Eu3+-TTA-CTAB-Gd3+. This shows that the presence of Cur has little effect on the local symmetry around Eu3+ ion in the Eu3+-TTA-CTAB-Gd3+ complex. Consequently, the fluorescence quenched of the Eu3+-TTA-CTAB-Gd3+ system by Cur should mainly result from the coordination of Cur with Gd3+, which impedes the intermolecular energy transfer from Gd3+-TTA to Eu3+. The impede action should be owing to the differences of the lowest triplet energy level of the TTA (20,400 cm−1) [33] and that of Cur (1.91eV [34] calculated to 15,390 cm−1). The variation tendency of the R at 746 nm wavelength excitation was basically consistent with that at 360 nm wavelength excitation. Nevertheless, the R value at 746 nm excitation was always smaller than that at 360 nm excitation. From Fig. 2C, under 746 nm excitation, the chromaticity change caused by Cur was larger than that under 360 nm excitation. In conclusion, there are obviously difference in the luminescence under 360 nm and 746 nm wavelength excitation, respectively.

The fluorescence decay curves of the Eu3+-TTA-CTAB-Gd3+system with Cur under 360 nm and 746 nm excitation wavelength are shown in Fig. S2. The average fluorescence lifetime of the system is calculated by the formula:
τ
a v e
=
∑
τ i
R e
l i
. The results showed that Cur could reduce the τ ave of Eu3+-TTA-CTAB-Gd3+ from 496.00 μs to 309.52 μs at λ ex = 360 nm and from 501.31 μs to 304.97 μs at λ ex = 746 nm, respectively. And the fluorescence quantum yields (Φ) of the systems under 360 nm and 746 nm excitation wavelength were also measured. The quantum yield values of Eu3+-TTA-CTAB-Gd3+ decreased from 17.47% to 8.27% at λ ex = 360 nm and from 0.18% to 0.03% at λ ex = 746 nm in the presence of Cur. The results show that the τ ave and Φ values of the complexes measured with two methods are markedly different. The changes in τ ave and Φ of the NIRL excitation fluorescence of Eu3+-TTA-CTAB-Gd3+ by Cur were bigger than that of the Stokes fluorescence. The decreases in τ ave and Φ values of the system are contributed to the quenching effect of Cur on the fluorescence of the Eu3+-TTA-CTAB-Gd3+. The results also indicate that there are obviously characteristic difference between the NIRL excitation fluorescence and the Stokes fluorescence of the Eu(III) complex.

In order to explore the interaction mechanism of the system, a series of measurements were carried out. UV–Vis absorption spectra are shown in Fig. 3 . The absorption peaks of TTA were located at 338 nm and 262 nm, which correspond to the π-π* transitions of the enol and keto form, respectively [35]. The absorbance of TTA at 338 nm was slightly reduced by Eu3+. While the absorbance showed obviously decrease with the addition of CTAB. The absorbance decreases should be due to the electronic cloud of TTA shift and the energy required for π-π* transition increases caused by the electrostatic interaction between TTA and CTAB [36]. The presence of Gd3+ mainly reduced the absorption of TTA at 262 nm and the peak at 338 nm was slightly reduced. While when Gd3+ was added to Eu3+-TTA-CTAB, the peaks at 262 nm and 338 nm both slightly red-shift and the absorption at 338 nm obviously decreased. Besides, a shoulder peak appeared at 380 nm and a wide-band absorption less than 600 nm was observed. The changes in the absorption intensities and profiles of the absorption revealed that the enol group of TTA can both coordinate to Eu3+ and Gd3+ ions and the presence of CTAB contributes to the reactions. In addition, the absorption peaks of Cur were located at 364 nm and 430 nm. The absorbance of Cur-CTAB at 364 nm was decreased and the absorption peak at 430 nm was red shifted to 435 nm, which proved that there may be interactions between CTAB and Cur. When Eu3+ and Gd3+ were added separately to the Cur solution, the notable difference of the absorbance was found. Eu3+ could lower the absorbance of Cur at 364 nm, and has less effect on the peak at 430 nm. While, when Gd3+ binds with Cur, the maximum absorbance at about 435 nm was observed. Compared to Eu3+, there is stronger coordination of Gd3+ with Cur. The new absorption peak of the Eu3+-Cur-TTA-CTAB-Gd3+ system located at about 460 nm, and the shoulder peak caused by Gd3+ have weakened. The result shows that Gd3+ is prefer to coordinate with Cur in the system, which lead to reduce its binding action with TTA. In addition, the absorbance of TTA at 338 nm was enhanced and was the highest among the systems, which may be the weakening of the hydrophobic effect of CTAB caused by the combination of Cur and CTAB. Therefore, the intermolecular energy transfer efficiency was reduced and the fluorescence intensity decreased.

CTAB has the function of structure directing agent and stabilizer [37]. To further demonstrate the effect of CTAB, the surface tensions of the system with various CTAB concentrations were measured. The plot of surface tension (σ) as a function of the logarithm of CTAB concentration is showed in Fig. 4 . The critical micelle concentration (CMC) of CTAB obtained from the intersection of the two fitted lines of σ was 6.5 × 10−4 mol/L, while the CMC of Eu3+-TTA-CTAB-Gd3+ and Eu3+-Cur-TTA-CTAB-Gd3+ decreased to 2.2 × 10−4 mol/L and 2.8 × 10−4 mol/L, respectively. Similar to Na+ [38], the presence of Eu3+ and Gd3+ could also reduce the CMC value of CTAB. The metal ions could reduce the electrostatic repulsion between the polar head groups, which led to the micelle of CTAB formed easily at low concentration. At lower CTAB concentrations, Cur resulted in a significant reduction in surface tension. This may be caused by the β-diketone structure of Cur combined with the head group of CTAB [39]. In addition, the CMC of Eu3+-TTA-CTAB-Gd3+ increased slightly with Cur presented, we think the Cur has influence on the formation of micelles of CTAB.

The resonance light scattering (RLS) spectra was also adopted to investigated the interaction mechanism of the system (Fig. 5 ). The maximum RLS peak of Eu3+-TTA complex was located at 505 nm. In the presence of CTAB, the peak intensity at 428 nm increased notably and was basically equal to that at 505 nm. The RLS bands around 505 nm and 428 nm should be mainly result from the coordination of Eu3+ with TTA and the solubilization of CTAB, respectively. In particularly, as Gd3+ was added in, two RLS peaks both gave the highest enhanced and their enhancement efficiencies are about same. In the presence of Gd3+, Gd3+-TTA-CTAB complex coexists with Eu3+-TTA-CTAB complex in the solution, the larger aggregates formed by the solubilization and immobilization effect of CTAB, led to greatly enhancement of the RLS intensity. However, the addition of Cur could lead to a remarkable decrease of the RLS intensity the Eu3+-TTA-CTAB-Gd3+ system. In other word, the extent of the RLS signal enhancement of the Eu3+-TTA-CTAB system was significant different caused by the Gd3+ with and without Cur. It is also found that the RLS intensity of Eu3+-Cur-CTAB-Gd3+ was very weak. The above results indicated a competitive interaction between Cur and TTA for combining with Gd3+. And then it is worth noting that the peak intensity of the Eu3+-Cur-TTA-CTAB-Gd3+ system at 428 nm was lower than that of at 505 nm. Owing to the RLS peak at about 428 nm is related to the action of CTAB, its obvious changes show that the interaction between Cur and CTAB should also be considered. In consequence, the interactions of Cur with CTAB and Gd3+ should be dominant factors of impeding intermolecular energy transfer of the system.

The effect of pH value on the Stokes fluorescence and the NIRL excitation fluorescence were tested (Fig. S3). At pH = 8.0, the reduction of fluorescence intensity ΔI was maximum. The effects of various buffer solutions were also measured at pH = 8.00 ± 0.05. The relative value of ΔI (%, λ ex = 746 nm) for HEPES-NaOH, barbitone sodium-HCl, tris(hydroxymethyl) aminomethane-HCl, hexamethylene-tetramine (HMTA)-HCl, Britton-Robinson (BR)-NaOH, Na2HPO4-Citric Acid were 100, 63, 92, 66, 0 and 0, respectively. In this work, 0.01 mol/L pH = 8.0 HEPES-NaOH buffer solution was chosen. The effects of various surfactants on the Eu(III) fluorescence were also monitored (Table S2). The ΔI absolute value (λ ex = 746 nm) of the Eu(III) complex with Cur in the presence of CTAB, cetylpyridinium bromide (CPB), emulsifier OP, sodium dodecyl benzene sulfonate (SDBS), sodium dodecyl sulfonate (SDS), sodium lauryl sulfate (SLS) were 810,532, 794,448, 11,226, 1290, 652, 228 and 807, respectively. The cationic surfactants gave greater fluorescence enhancement than non-ionic or anionic surfactants. And the Eu3+ complex system with CTAB had the greatest fluorescence enhancement, and the strongest fluorescence reduction ΔI was obtained after adding Cur. The concentrations of Eu3+, TTA, CTAB and Gd3+ were optimized (Figs. S4-S7). As Eu3+ concentration fixed at 5.0 × 10−7 mol/L, the ΔI maximum value could be obtained under the conditions of 4.0 × 10−5 mol/L TTA, 1.0 × 10−4 mol/L CTAB and 7.0 × 10−6 mol/L Gd3+. The optimum adding sequence was Eu3+, Cur, TTA, CTAB, HEPES-NaOH, Gd3+ and the fluorescence signals were recorded after incubation for 50 min.

Under the optimum experimental conditions, the analytical performance of the proposed two methods for detecting Cur are shown in Fig. 7 . The error bars in linear relationship curves represent standard deviations of the triplicates. For the NIRL excitation fluorescence method, the linear range of Cur was obtained from 2.0 × 10−9 mol/L to 6.0 × 10−8 mol/L, R 2 = 0.9961, the limit of detection (LOD) was 5.2 × 10−10 mol/L (S/N = 3), the limit of quantitation (LOQ) was 1.7 × 10−9 mol/L (S/N = 10). For the Stokes fluorescence method, the reduction of fluorescence intensity ΔI had a good linear correlation with the Cur concentration in the range of 1.0 × 10−8 mol/L - 6.0 × 10−8 mol/L, R 2 = 0.9985, LOD = 2.6 × 10−9 mol/L (S/N = 3), LOQ = 8.5 × 10−9 mol/L (S/N = 10). The linear range, LOD and LOQ of the NIRL excitation fluorescence method are superior to those of the Stokes fluorescence method. The linear range and LOD of the two proposed methods and some reported methods are listed in Table 2 . Compared with most reported methods, the two proposed methods have a lower LOD, they have an advantage in detection sensitivity.

The applicability of the methods was demonstrated, we applied the two proposed methods to determine Cur in commercially available ginger powder samples by standard addition method (Table 3 ). Different amounts of Cur standard solution were added to the 100-fold diluted ginger powder extraction solution (0.20 mL) and diluted to 10 mL. The recovery rates of Cur were in the range of 93.5%–98.5% and 92.3%–95.4% for the Stokes fluorescence methods and NIRL excitation fluorescence methods, respectively. The two proposed methods are of applicability for the analysis of actual samples.

In this work, we found that Eu3+-TTA-CTAB-Gd3+ system could emission the Eu(III) intrinsic fluorescence under the NIRL excitation of xenon lamp. The excitation wavelengths were distributed in a wide range from 710 nm to 790 nm. The luminescence excited at 746 nm was mainly the SODL excitation fluorescence. Cur could quench the fluorescence of Eu3+-TTA-CTAB-Gd3+ complex under 360 nm and 746 nm excitation, respectively. The interaction between Cur and Gd3+ inhibited the coordination between Gd3+ and TTA, and then the intermolecular energy transfer was weakened due to the poor energy level matching between Cur and Eu3+. In addition, there were differences in fluorescence intensity, chromaticity, local symmetry around Eu3+ ion, fluorescence lifetime and quantum yield between the NIRL excitation fluorescence and the Stokes fluorescence of the system. The NIRL excitation fluorescence method shows some advantages over the Stokes fluorescence method with higher sensitivity and wider linear range. We hope that the NIRL excitation fluorescence method under Xe lamp can be used widely in the field of analysis.

Fangzheng Yuan: Conceptualization, Methodology, Validation, Formal analysis, Investigation, Data curation, Writing - original draft, Writing - review & editing, Visualization. Xia Wu: Conceptualization, Methodology, Resources, Writing - review & editing, Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition. Hui Zhang: Writing - review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (grant numbers 21545001, 21475076) and Natural Science Foundation of Shandong Province, China (grant number ZR2018MB031).
